title: The First Sunday after the Epiphany: Baptism of our Lord
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
January 9, 2022 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the First Sunday after the Epiphany, observing the Feast of the Baptism of Our Lord, falling eight days after Epiphany (January 6). The Revised Common Lectionary readings would have included Isaiah 42:5-9 (God's servant chosen), Psalm 29 (God's voice upon the waters), Acts 10:34-43 (Peter's proclamation at Cornelius's house), and Matthew 3:13-17 (Christ's baptism by John). The church calendar is in the Christmas-Epiphany season, transitioning between the liturgical celebration of Jesus's birth and the revelation of his identity, before entering Ordinary Time after Epiphany.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in early January 2022 was navigating ongoing divisions over sexuality and biblical interpretation following the church's 2021 General Convention, which had passed resolutions affirming LGBTQ+ inclusion and rejecting traditional teachings on marriage. The Diocese of Colorado was part of the broader Anglican Communion's tensions, with some parishes and clergy continuing to press for traditional doctrine while others embraced the church's recent progressive stance. No specific diocesan events are documented for this exact week, though the theological climate remained contentious in many parishes nationwide.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical January weather with snow and cold temperatures affecting the region. No major local crises or exceptional community events are documented for the week immediately preceding January 9, 2022. The city continued managing COVID-19 pandemic protocols, which were still in effect as the Omicron variant spread rapidly across Colorado in early January.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The United States was consumed by the one-year anniversary of the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot, with commemorative coverage and congressional investigations dominating news cycles the week of January 9. The nation was experiencing a significant COVID-19 surge driven by the Omicron variant, with record case numbers and hospitalizations straining healthcare systems nationwide. The Department of Justice continued high-profile arrests and prosecutions related to the Capitol insurrection, with over 700 individuals charged at this point.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia was massing troops on Ukraine's border with an estimated 100,000+ military personnel positioned for a potential invasion, though Russian officials denied aggressive intentions. China was weeks away from the Beijing Winter Olympics (February 4-20, 2022), which would become a focus of diplomatic tensions over human rights concerns. The Middle East continued experiencing instability with Houthi attacks on Saudi Arabia and ongoing conflict in Yemen and Syria.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America was entering 2022 with a sense of pandemic fatigue mixed with anxiety about political polarization and the approaching midterm elections. Despite Omicron's severity, most major venues and institutions had reopened, though masking and vaccination remained divisive cultural markers. Sports fans were anticipating the Super Bowl (February 13, 2022), with the Denver Broncos remaining notably absent from playoff contention, a source of local sports frustration.
